Welcome to the=C2=A01,800=C2=A0newly Not Boring people=C2=A0who have joined=
 us since last Monday! If you haven=E2=80=99t subscribed, join=C2=A0139,616=
 smart, curious folks by subscribing here:
=F0=9F=8E=A7 The audio version is back! To listen to me read, head over to =
Spotify [https://substack.com/redirect/79e2e2be-a782-490a-9e81-82247cf556d6=
?u=3D16283549] or=C2=A0Apple Podcasts [https://substack.com/redirect/cccce8=
48-4097-4740-ab9a-dc86df6f0107?u=3D16283549].
Hi friends =F0=9F=91=8B,=C2=A0
Happy Monday! As sometimes happens, over the weekend, I got walloped with a=
 case of Writer=E2=80=99s Block. I couldn=E2=80=99t figure out a topic. Nor=
mally, I=E2=80=99ll head into the weekend with at least a rough idea of wha=
t I want to write about, but this weekend: blankness. I stared at the compu=
ter screen. I turned to Twitter [https://substack.com/redirect/0dcbe455-cd8=
f-43fc-af17-5d3052958f22?u=3D16283549]. I looked at the whiteboard on which=
 I write potential future ideas, and got bored by all of them. I started wr=
iting a piece on The Bear and the Hard Work War, and then I stopped, 1,266 =
words in.=C2=A0
What=E2=80=99s in the news right now that would make a compelling piece? Tw=
itter? I=E2=80=99ve written about it enough, and Matt Levine is doing Hall =
of Fame work there [https://substack.com/redirect/b78caeec-860b-4add-8d6c-2=
b68dd3c2f8a?u=3D16283549]. The Fed? That=E2=80=99s been covered by much sma=
rter people, no one knows what=E2=80=99s going to happen, and Arthur Hayes =
wrote this banger [https://substack.com/redirect/1cdba8c1-bda9-48b8-8fa9-a1=
dbc11ca4f0?u=3D16283549].
Sometimes, you just don=E2=80=99t have it. Realizing that this was one of t=
hose times was freeing. Instead of trying to do a deep dive or a bunch of a=
nalysis, I decided to let my imagination run wild (and keep it brief). It=
=E2=80=99s summer, things are slow=E2=80=A6 let=E2=80=99s have a little fun=
=2E=C2=A0
Let=E2=80=99s get to it.
Today=E2=80=99s Not Boring is brought to you by=E2=80=A6 Arrived [https://s=
ubstack.com/redirect/26603eed-65fb-479c-9f69-b8f150bfd46c?u=3D16283549]
The bulk of the time we spend managing our money is boring. Paying bills - =
boring. Filing your taxes - boring. Budgeting - boring.
You know what isn=E2=80=99t boring? Building the foundation of your financi=
al future using one of the most tried and true ways - investing in resident=
ial rental properties for the steady price appreciation and passive income =
the asset class offers. However, buying a rental property comes with high u=
pfront costs and operational headaches like sourcing tenants and maintainin=
g the property, which tend to push investors back to =E2=80=9Ceasier=E2=80=
=9D asset classes.
That=E2=80=99s the problem Arrived [https://substack.com/redirect/26603eed-=
65fb-479c-9f69-b8f150bfd46c?u=3D16283549] set out to solve in 2019.
With Arrived [https://substack.com/redirect/26603eed-65fb-479c-9f69-b8f150b=
fd46c?u=3D16283549], you can buy shares of individual rental homes that wer=
e handpicked by their in-house experts based on their long-term appreciatio=
n and income potential. They also take care of the operations and property =
management so you can kick back, relax, and watch your wealth grow - defini=
tely not boring.
Sign up is simple, minimums are flexible, and the portfolio diversification=
 is *chef=E2=80=99s kiss*.
Get started with Arrived [https://substack.com/redirect/26603eed-65fb-479c-=
9f69-b8f150bfd46c?u=3D16283549] today.

One of the things that makes the future so hard to predict is that new tech=
nologies don=E2=80=99t operate in silos; they=E2=80=99re intertwining threa=
ds that weave together in unpredictable ways.=C2=A0
Uber is a canonical weave: for anyone to be able to dial up a ride from the=
ir pocket, GPS, smartphones, payments, and online ratings systems needed to=
 combine in ways that their respective inventors couldn=E2=80=99t have pred=
icted. This conversation between Patrick O=E2=80=99Shaughnessy and Kenneth =
Stanley [https://substack.com/redirect/477be772-2ce5-4d03-998d-fddb03fe4389=
?u=3D16283549] is rich with examples of discrete innovations combining in w=
ays that couldn=E2=80=99t be planned, like the invention of the vacuum tube=
 unintentionally leading to the computer.
As the individual technological threads each get wilder and more powerful t=
han they were in previous generations =E2=80=93 replace =E2=80=9Cvacuum tub=
es=E2=80=9D with =E2=80=9CAI=E2=80=9D as an input, for example =E2=80=93 I =
suspect that the remixed outputs will be even harder to predict.
Even in sci-fi, where the job is to imagine fictional futures by extrapolat=
ing technological progress, most of the technology is fairly linear extrapo=
lation: fusion-powered spaceships, AI robots, terraforming. I think we=E2=
=80=99ll have all of that at some point, but I also think we=E2=80=99ll hav=
e all sorts of crazy combinations that are harder to predict, and then we=
=E2=80=99ll have crazier combinations of those crazy combinations.=C2=A0
To show you what I mean, we=E2=80=99ll walk through some simple examples (w=
ith the caveat that these are just some of the ones I can dream up; the cro=
wd will create things that make these look quaint).
Over the weekend, I came across this thread:=C2=A0
When I first saw OpenAI [https://substack.com/redirect/bd520c05-abcc-4059-9=
3d3-32190a20b35e?u=3D16283549]=E2=80=99s DALL=E2=80=A2E 2 in the wild [http=
s://substack.com/redirect/0f8d5498-cf34-4f4f-b51d-0ee93c3fcf4c?u=3D16283549=
] a few months ago, the first thing I thought was, =E2=80=9CWoah.=E2=80=9D =
The second thing I thought was, =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s going to be really wi=
ld when this gets combined with other technology.=E2=80=9D I started jottin=
g down notes for an essay on combining technologies, then I abandoned it fo=
r a couple months until I saw Nicholas Ptacek=E2=80=99s tweet.=C2=A0
Nicholas=E2=80=99 experiment was one I=E2=80=99ve wanted to run: ask GPT-3 =
[https://substack.com/redirect/db90ed78-3a81-4494-a0ae-c1721a1bc35b?u=3D162=
83549] to write prompts for DALL=E2=80=A2E 2. Ask the AI to ask the other A=
I to create something.=C2=A0
Using outputs from one OpenAI system as an input for another is an on-the-n=
ose example of composability [https://substack.com/redirect/aa748776-c2c7-4=
d82-9ce7-d0bc882af257?u=3D16283549], combining one lego block with another,=
 even manually, to create something new. It=E2=80=99s not hard to imagine t=
hat someone will automate this, so that I can type in a random idea, GPT-3 =
creates a bunch of DALL=E2=80=A2E 2 prompts from it, and DALL=E2=80=A2E 2 s=
pins up images based on those prompts.=C2=A0
But that=E2=80=99s just the first and most obvious step here. Presumably, t=
he models will continue to get better and be able to do more things. Ben Th=
ompson wrote an excellent piece on this idea: DALL-E, the Metaverse, and Ze=
ro Marginal Content [https://substack.com/redirect/33135d75-bead-4d74-99ef-=
2d2364dd97c7?u=3D16283549].
DALL=E2=80=A2E 13 might be able to create short films from scripts generate=
d by GPT-11.
I=E2=80=99m sure that there are all sorts of things that make this many ord=
ers of magnitude harder than creating static images (which itself is very h=
ard)=E2=80=A6
=E2=80=A6 and I=E2=80=99m equally sure that, at some point, it will be a re=
ality. I=E2=80=99ll probably create and watch some of those short films mys=
elf; Dev certainly will; his kids will find them terribly old-fashioned.
Zoom forward a little further. Imagine combining DALL=E2=80=A2E 22, GPT-19,=
 and Snap Spectacles 15 [https://substack.com/redirect/93114024-ac84-4fcf-a=
50f-dba90419e8dc?u=3D16283549]. Then remember that Snap recently acquired [=
https://substack.com/redirect/ef63ec6e-ef80-45a9-9d49-f23205572021?u=3D1628=
3549] brain-computer interface startup NextMind.=C2=A0
Dev=E2=80=99s kids will be thinking things like =E2=80=9Cspace battle jungl=
e=E2=80=9D (they won=E2=80=99t know what a =E2=80=9Cvideo game=E2=80=9D is)=
 and will pop into a full-blown, freshly-created AR/VR game of their imagin=
ation.=C2=A0=C2=A0
Dev will be lucky to have something to keep his kids entertained. The fligh=
t to his family=E2=80=99s Lunar vacation will be shorter than the current t=
hree days it takes to get to the Moon, but parents will always do whatever =
it takes to keep their kids from screaming on flights.=C2=A0
Pull on just a few of the many open tech threads, and you land in a world i=
n which anyone can create their own entertainment instead of passively cons=
uming it, at the speed of thought. It will make TikTok look slow and archai=
c, for better and for worse.=C2=A0
But that=E2=80=99s just one pair of threads. The world won=E2=80=99t, I hop=
e, look like the DALL=E2=80=A2E version of that scene in Wall-E.=C2=A0
As Matt Ball pointed out on Derek Thompson=E2=80=99s Plain English [https:/=
/substack.com/redirect/87798d32-a64e-4bd9-945d-08a41fe59b1f?u=3D16283549], =
the time Dev=E2=80=99s kids spend in the Metaverse will hopefully replace t=
he 3-5 hours per day of =E2=80=9Clean-back=E2=80=9D TV consumption with som=
ething more engaging and creative.=C2=A0
But I want my grandkids to spend more time outside, in nature, too. A diffe=
rent, and eventually intersecting, track is one proposed by Not Boring=E2=
=80=99s own Elliot Hershberg yesterday in Viriditas [https://substack.com/r=
edirect/4a621170-921f-4aeb-b5b3-9c050f4bd07d?u=3D16283549].
Laying out his personal mission =E2=80=93 =E2=80=9CI want life to flourish =
in the universe. I view biotechnology as the most logical means towards thi=
s end.=E2=80=9D =E2=80=93 Elliot makes the case for more biological growth:=
=C2=A0
Growing trees, the living systems that pull CO2 out of the atmosphere while=
 turning sunlight and water into beautiful three dimensional structures tha=
t carpet large portions of our planet. Growing food [https://substack.com/r=
edirect/2211a7bd-23d6-4af8-aec6-bbfa1cb62701?u=3D16283549] using the awesom=
e power of microbes in a way that could sustainably feed the entire planet.=
 Growing our medicines=E2=80=94advanced nucleic acid, cell-based, and micro=
bial therapies that enable us to live healthier and longer lives. Growing m=
aterials [https://substack.com/redirect/47e47102-ac17-40cf-87d2-2d531bb3678=
f?u=3D16283549] to build advanced cities with abundant housing.
Instead of escaping into virtual worlds, at some point, we=E2=80=99ll be ab=
le to grow new ones. Imagine Dev=E2=80=99s kids dreaming up the ideal treeh=
ouse =E2=80=A6 and then growing it in their backyard.=C2=A0
To keep pulling on threads=E2=80=A6
Thanks to Dan [https://substack.com/redirect/ab9051bb-2304-4325-8b1b-15ef5f=
a08cf8?u=3D16283549] for editing!
Have a great week, and see you back here on Thursday!=20
Thanks for reading,
Packy
